Case Study

Designing trust into a service that sends strangers to your home when you're sick.

Drect is an on demand doctor app for Dubai. Freelance project. A client handed me 6 incomplete wireframes covering 24% of the product. I designed the other 76%, solo, in 30 days.

Role

Product Designer (Solo)

Timeline

30 days

The problem

You're sick. You open Drect. Pick a specialty. The system assigns the nearest available doctor. They drive to your door. You pay cash after the consultation. That's the product.

The client had the concept and a developer ready. What they didn't have was a product. They sent me 6 wireframes: a login screen, an OTP screen, a home screen with three identical "General Physician" labels, a Google Maps screenshot, a booking screen with placeholder copy, and a profile screen. That was supposed to be the entire app.

The core design challenge wasn't making these screens look better. It was figuring out what the product actually needed. How does doctor assignment work? What happens between tapping "Book" and the doctor arriving? What if the service isn't available in your area? None of these questions had answers in the wireframes.

The wireframes covered about 24% of the screens the app needed. I had to design the other 76% from scratch, including the entire booking flow, all edge cases, the post visit experience, and the information architecture that ties it all together.

Framing the design challenge

Before opening Figma, I spent the first few days just asking questions and mapping what was missing. Four things became clear:

Trust is the product

Users are letting a stranger into their home when they're vulnerable. Every screen needs to build confidence: real doctor photos, visible credentials, live tracking, transparent pricing.

Speed, not choice

This isn't a marketplace. The system picks the nearest doctor. Designing a browsing experience would have been the wrong solution. The UX needed to feel like Uber: tap and go.

The map carries the experience

Availability, assignment, tracking, arrival. The map communicates all of it. I made it the dominant element on every booking screen.

Edge cases define quality

What if the service isn't in your area? Server down? Doctor's name overflows the card? These aren't afterthoughts. They're what separates a mockup from a shippable product.

These four principles shaped every design decision. When I had to choose between showing more doctor info or keeping the booking flow fast, "speed not choice" gave me the answer. When I wasn't sure whether to add credentials to the tracking screen, "trust is the product" made it obvious.

Structuring the product

The wireframes didn't have an information architecture. So I built one. This is the complete product structure showing what existed in the wireframes (grey), what I designed from scratch (blue), and what edge cases I added (amber).

Designed from scratch
Edge case / error state
Entry point

In wireframes

Login / Register selection
Phone number input (UAE +971)

OTP verification (4 digit)
OTP states: partial, error, resend timer
02

I designed
Custom permission prompt
"Enter location manually" fallback
Location denied: manual entry required
Not available in your area

In wireframes
Map with nearby doctor pins
Location dropdown with change option
Time selector: "Now" or schedule later
Category tabs: General, Pediatric, Physio

"Book Doctor" primary CTA

Search bar, saved addresses, recent searches

Date + time picker bottom sheet

In wireframes

Add patient sub flow (name, age, gender)
Add address sub flow (flat, building, type)
"Assigning shortly..." animation
Doctor assigned: photo, credentials, ETA
Real time tracking on map
"Doctor reached home" confirmation
Long doctor name truncation
Bottom tab: Profile (Tab 2)

In wireframes
User card, addresses, patients, reports
My bookings
Booking list with status badges
Empty state: "No bookings yet"
Booking detail (post visit) I designed
Visit header, doctor info, rating
Price breakdown, invoice download
Doctor's notes, medications, lab tests
Follow up scheduling

I designed
Photo, specialization, stats, reviews
System states (global)

I designed
Server error + "Retry"
Not serviceable + "Change location"
Skeleton loading state
Empty bookings illustration
Input validation errors
Long name truncation
Total screens: 50+
From wireframes: ~12 (24%)
Designed from scratch: ~38 (76%)
Edge cases: 15+

Key design decisions

The home screen took 4 iterations to get right

This is the most important screen in the app. It's where you book a doctor. The wireframe had three identical "General Physician" labels on a map. The problem: how do you let users pick a specialty without turning it into a slow marketplace experience?

I tried icon chips (too minimal, users didn't know what each specialty treats or costs), full card lists (too heavy, buried the map and killed the Uber feel), and tabs with a single card (close, but the map felt disconnected from the categories). The final version uses horizontal tabs for switching specialties, one detail card with pricing and symptoms, and live doctor pins on the map showing real availability. Map stays dominant. Booking stays fast.

The booking flow didn't exist. I designed all 6 screens.

The wireframes jumped from "Book Doctor" to "Doctor Assigned" with nothing in between. But that middle part is the entire experience. I designed the confirm details screen (which handles first time vs returning users differently), the "assigning you a doctor shortly" loading state (because without it users don't know if the app is working), the doctor assigned screen with photo, credentials, and ETA (because trust), the live tracking map (because anxiety), and the "Doctor reached home" confirmation (because closure). Each screen was designed to answer a specific user anxiety at that moment in the flow.

Before and after

Same three screens. Completely different products. The home screen went from three text labels to a map first interface with live doctor availability. The booking screen went from a plain text list to a real time tracking experience. The profile went from a basic settings page to a health management hub with booking history, addresses, patients, and reports.

I designed 15+ edge cases nobody asked for

This is where most projects fall apart. The happy path is easy. But what does the user see when they deny location access? When the service isn't available in their city? When the server goes down? When they open "My Bookings" and there's nothing there? I designed every one of these because if you don't, developers either make something up or ship a blank screen. Neither is acceptable.

Impact

Defined the product model

The wireframes didn't specify how doctor assignment works. I designed the auto assignment flow, which became the product's core differentiator from marketplace apps. A design decision that shaped the engineering architecture.

Designed for trust at every step

Doctor photo, credentials, live map, and ETA visible from assignment to arrival. Every screen in the booking flow was designed to reduce the anxiety of letting a stranger into your home.

Zero developer guesswork

50+ screens, each with all states designed: empty, loading, error, success. Devs could build without coming back to ask "what should this look like when..."

Expanded the product scope

Designed the entire post visit experience (notes, medications, lab results, invoices, follow ups) that wasn't in the brief. Turned a booking app into a health management app.

6 incomplete wireframes to 50+ production screens. 30 days. Solo designer. No PM, no researcher, no existing design system. I went from ambiguous wireframes to a complete, developer ready product. Designs were handed off to development. The product has not launched yet.

Reflection

The best work I did on this project wasn't visual. It was asking the right questions early enough that the answers could shape the product. The wireframes were incomplete, but that turned out to be the opportunity. It meant I got to define how the product works, not just what it looks like.

Should have pushed for user research

I relied on assumptions and client conversations instead of talking to actual users in Dubai. That's the one thing I'd change.
